title: Tokenized Deposits & Stablecoins: The New Battleground for Corporate Treasury Banking
summary: The emergence of bank-issued tokenized deposits — led by JPMorgan's Kinexis platform and the TCH Regulated Liability Network pilot — marks a structural shift in how commercial bank money moves. Combined with the entry of non-bank stablecoin players into cross-border B2B payments (MoneyGram, Western Union), treasury-serving banks face simultaneous threats to their highest-margin wholesale payment and liquidity products and a narrowing window to build equivalent capabilities before regulatory moats erode.
businessImpact: Banks that do not have a tokenized deposit or programmable liquidity strategy within 18 months risk being competitively disadvantaged in institutional and large corporate treasury relationships. The window is partially protected by current regulatory frameworks that favor bank-issued tokenized deposits — but this advantage is time-limited as GENIUS Act implementation progresses.
body:
## The Landscape

Three distinct developments are converging to reshape the payment and liquidity stack for corporate treasury banking.

**1. Bank-Issued Tokenized Deposits (JPM Kinexis, TCH RLN)**

JPMorgan's Kinexis platform (formerly Onyx/JPM Coin) now processes over $1 billion in daily institutional transactions, enabling programmable intraday liquidity, automated repo settlement, and 24/7 cross-border USD/EUR transfers on a permissioned blockchain. The platform is live with a select group of institutional clients and is expanding to include corporate treasury clients for intraday cash management.

In parallel, The Clearing House is piloting the Regulated Liability Network — a shared ledger where tokenized commercial bank money from multiple institutions can be exchanged without converting to central bank money. If successful, RLN could become the settlement layer for a next-generation interbank payment system operating parallel to RTP and FedNow.

**2. Non-Bank Stablecoin Players Entering B2B (MoneyGram, Western Union)**

MoneyGram's stablecoin-settled cross-border B2B platform — built on USDC and the Stellar network — now covers 20 markets at settlement speeds under 3 minutes and fees substantially below correspondent banking rates. Western Union's B2B digital platform targets mid-market corporate treasury clients with API-first ERP integration and competitive FX pricing.

These are not peripheral fintech plays — they are established brands with global networks actively commoditizing correspondent banking fees.

**3. Regulatory Framework Clarification**

The Federal Reserve's 2025 supervisory guidance draws a clear line between bank-issued tokenized deposits (permissible under existing charter authority, subject to reserve and liquidity requirements) and non-bank payment stablecoins (subject to separate licensing under the proposed GENIUS Act). This creates a temporary regulatory moat for chartered banks.

## Competitive Threat Matrix

| Product / Revenue Stream | Threat Actor | Threat Level | Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intraday liquidity / credit lines | JPM Kinexis | High | Now |
| Interbank settlement fees | TCH RLN | Medium | 12-24 months |
| Cross-border wire fees (mid-market) | MoneyGram, Western Union | High | Now |
| FX conversion revenue | Western Union B2B | Medium | 6-18 months |
| Repo / collateral management | JPM Kinexis | Medium-High | Now |

## Strategic Implications

**For Treasury Product Teams:**
The most immediate action is a client segmentation analysis — identifying which institutional and corporate treasury clients currently using traditional intraday credit, repo, or cross-border wire services are also being actively marketed to by Kinexis or the non-bank platforms. These are the highest-risk relationships.

**For Risk Teams:**
As stablecoin-settled transactions grow, traditional transaction monitoring frameworks will need to adapt. Stablecoin flows may not appear in standard correspondent banking reporting, creating potential gaps in AML/sanctions screening coverage.

**For Product Strategy:**
The regulatory window — where bank-issued tokenized deposits have clear legal standing that non-bank stablecoins do not — should be treated as a product development sprint, not a waiting period. Banks that have a tokenized deposit product in limited pilot within 12 months will be positioned to market it as a regulated, insured alternative to non-bank stablecoins when the GENIUS Act licensing regime becomes clearer.

## Sources
JPMorgan Chase Kinexis platform disclosures; The Clearing House RLN pilot documentation; Federal Reserve supervisory guidance on tokenized deposits (2025); Finextra MoneyGram coverage; PYMNTS Western Union B2B reporting; BIS working paper on tokenized money (2024); FSB report on crypto-asset market developments (2025).
technicalTakeaway: Permissioned blockchain settlement enables atomic DVP settlement and 24/7 operations that traditional RTGS/ACH infrastructure cannot replicate without multi-year infrastructure programs.
businessTakeaway: The regulatory moat protecting bank-issued tokenized deposits from non-bank competition is real but time-limited. Speed to market with a tokenized deposit product is a strategic imperative.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasury teams at banks not offering tokenized liquidity management will increasingly see institutional clients ask for it explicitly in RFP processes — the product gap will become a sales liability.

title: The Real-Time Payments Inflection Point: Where the Hockey Stick Actually Comes From
summary: RTP and FedNow adoption has followed a linear, not exponential, growth curve since launch — despite strong supply-side network growth. The analysis in this deep dive identifies three structural barriers preventing volume inflection and maps the specific product investments banks need to make to capture disproportionate share of the volume hockey stick when it arrives. The window to position is now: the technical barriers are solvable, and the bank that solves them first for corporate treasury clients captures the relationship.
businessImpact: The volume hockey stick in real-time payments will not come from consumer use cases or from simply being on the network. It will come from banks that solve three specific corporate-side barriers: ERP integration, Request for Payment workflow enablement, and a clear ACH-displacement ROI model. Banks that solve these first for corporate treasury clients will capture the lion's share of the coming volume inflection and the deepened treasury relationships that follow.
body:
## Where We Are

FedNow crossed 1,000 participating institutions in 2025, with transaction volume growing 340% year-over-year from a low base. RTP is setting monthly volume records, with B2B use cases now representing over 60% of transaction value — up from 35% in 2023.

But the numbers tell a more nuanced story. The majority of FedNow-participating institutions are receive-only. RTP's $1M transaction limit continues to exclude the highest-value B2B flows. And the same three barriers that were cited in 2023 RFP assessments are still cited in 2025: no ERP integration, no RfP workflow, no ACH displacement model.

## The Three Barriers

**Barrier 1: ERP Integration Gap**

The majority of mid-market and large corporate AP teams initiate payments from Oracle NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, or Microsoft Dynamics. None of these have certified, out-of-the-box RTP or FedNow payment modules that allow payment initiation directly from the ERP without manual file-based batching. Until a corporate treasury team can initiate a real-time payment from within their existing payment workflow — without exporting a file, importing it to a banking portal, and manually approving — real-time payments remain an alternative channel, not a primary one.

Digital Transactions' analysis quantifies this: ERP integration is cited as a barrier by 71% of corporate treasury teams that evaluated but did not adopt real-time payments.

**Barrier 2: Request for Payment (RfP) Workflow Absence**

The highest-volume consumer use case for real-time payments in markets that have achieved hockey-stick growth (UK Faster Payments, India UPI, Brazil PIX) is not push payments — it's pull-initiated payments through a request framework. In the U.S., NACHA's RfP standard exists but has not been adopted by sufficient banks and billers to create network utility.

For corporate B2B applications, RfP enables suppliers to send payment requests directly into a buyer's ERP/TMS — eliminating invoice-to-payment matching friction and accelerating payment initiation from 3-5 days to same-day. Banks that enable RfP for corporate clients can pitch a working capital optimization story, not just a payment speed story.

**Barrier 3: Absence of ACH Displacement ROI Model**

The fee economics of real-time payments are not obviously favorable to corporate treasury clients on a per-transaction basis — ACH costs less, and batching is optimized in existing workflows. Banks have not developed and presented a total cost of ownership (TCO) model that quantifies the working capital value of ACH displacement for specific use cases.

The ROI exists. For a company with $500M in annual payables running on 2-day ACH, shifting to same-day or real-time payments frees approximately $2.7M in working capital annually (at 5% cost of capital). Banks that quantify this for specific client situations close deals; banks that pitch speed do not.

## What the Hockey Stick Requires

1. **Certified ERP connectors** for Oracle, SAP, and Microsoft Dynamics that enable direct real-time payment initiation from existing corporate workflows — no portal, no file export.

2. **RfP enablement** for at least the top 20 industry verticals where supplier-buyer payment friction is highest (manufacturing, healthcare, professional services, construction).

3. **Quantified ACH displacement models** built by industry segment that can be delivered by relationship managers in first and second client conversations.

## Competitive Positioning

The bank that executes this first in a given corporate client segment creates a deeply entrenched position. Once a corporate treasury team's ERP is configured for real-time payment initiation through a specific bank's API, migrating to a competitor requires a full TMS/ERP re-integration project — an 18-24 month undertaking that corporate treasury teams avoid without a compelling reason.

This is not a technology race — it's an integration and distribution race. The banks that win are not necessarily the ones with the most sophisticated real-time payment infrastructure; they are the ones that get their integration deepest into corporate clients' existing financial systems.

## Sources
FedNow Service participation data (2025); The Clearing House RTP volume reports (Q1 2025); Digital Transactions 'Where's the Hockey Stick?' analysis (May 2025); McKinsey Global Payments 2025; NACHA Same-Day ACH volume report; Fiserv 'Now' platform launch documentation; Oliver Wyman B2B payments research (2024).
technicalTakeaway: ERP-native payment initiation is the technical key to unlocking corporate volume. ISO 20022 structured data enables straight-through reconciliation — the actual ROI driver for corporate AP/AR teams.
businessTakeaway: The integration-first bank wins the treasury relationship and creates switching costs that protect the account for 5+ years.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasury teams should measure ACH displacement ROI before evaluating real-time rail adoption — the working capital case is almost always positive and is rarely quantified by bank partners proactively.
